departments.name: Quantitative Research
departments.mandate: Develop, test, document, and maintain systematic alpha signals, factor models, and portfolio-construction inputs under institutional-grade research standards (reproducibility, out-of-sample discipline, overfitting controls, model documentation), and operate the shared backtesting/research platform on which all systematic research at the firm runs.
responsibilities: Own the full signal research lifecycle: hypothesis formation, point-in-time data preparation, in-sample development, out-of-sample and walk-forward validation, peer review, production approval, and decommissioning
Build and maintain factor models (risk and alpha factors) used for portfolio construction, hedging, and performance attribution
Set and enforce backtesting standards: point-in-time data (no look-ahead/survivorship bias), realistic transaction-cost and market-impact assumptions, capacity analysis, and regime/stress evaluation

Enforce overfitting controls: a research trial registry that counts the number of strategy variants tested, multiple-testing corrections (e.g., Deflated Sharpe Ratio, Probability of Backtest Overfitting), purged/embargoed cross-validation or walk-forward testing, and mandatory holdout data
Produce SR 11-7-style model documentation for every production model: purpose, theory, data, assumptions, limitations, alternative approaches considered, testing results, and known failure modes
Maintain the firm model inventory entries for investment models (owner, risk tier, validation status, approval date) and support independent validation
Monitor production models: live-vs-backtest outcomes analysis, alpha decay tracking, drawdown and turnover diagnostics, and trigger-based review/retirement
Curate research datasets (market, reference, alternative, and on-chain data) with versioned, immutable snapshots so results are reproducible
Support compliant presentation of any backtested/hypothetical performance that leaves the firm (SEC Marketing Rule requirements)

workflows.name: Signal research lifecycle
workflows.description: Idea intake -> written hypothesis registered in trial registry -> point-in-time data prep -> in-sample development -> out-of-sample/walk-forward validation with multiple-testing-adjusted statistics (e.g., Deflated Sharpe Ratio) -> peer review / research committee challenge -> model documentation -> approval -> paper trading or limited-capital incubation -> production release.
inputs: Prior research library and trial registry
outputs: Backtest report with in-sample/out-of-sample statistics and trial counts
Production-ready signal code under version control

workflows.name: Production model monitoring and outcomes analysis
workflows.cadence: Daily automated monitoring; formal monthly review; risk-tiered annual revalidation
workflows.description: Compare live performance against backtest expectations (outcomes analysis per SR 11-7), track alpha decay, slippage vs assumed costs, factor exposure drift; escalate breaches to research committee and model risk; annual (or risk-tiered) revalidation of material models by an independent party.

workflows.name: Factor model refresh
workflows.cadence: Monthly/quarterly estimation refresh; annual methodology review
workflows.description: Re-estimate factor loadings and covariances, review factor definitions, test for factor crowding/decay, and publish updated models to risk and portfolio construction.
outputs: Updated factor model files consumed by risk and PM systems

workflows.name: Research platform and data governance
workflows.cadence: Quarterly platform review; continuous change management
workflows.description: Govern the shared backtesting environment: dataset versioning, environment reproducibility (containerized/pinned dependencies), code review and CI for research libraries, separation of research and production environments, access controls on holdout data.

workflows.name: Research review committee
workflows.description: Cross-team forum providing effective challenge on new signals, methodology changes, and promotion decisions; maintains minutes as governance evidence.

controls: Model risk management framework modeled on Fed SR 11-7 / OCC 2011-12 (voluntarily adopted buy-side): sound development, effective challenge via independent validation, and board/management-level governance
Independent validation of material models by staff not responsible for development or use (second line); validation frequency risk-tiered
Firm-wide model inventory with owners, risk tiers, and validation status
Trial registry / multiple-testing accounting so reported Sharpe ratios can be deflated for selection bias (Bailey & Lopez de Prado)
Mandatory out-of-sample/holdout discipline with access controls preventing repeated peeking at holdout data
Version control, code review, CI, and immutable point-in-time data snapshots for reproducibility

SEC Marketing Rule (Rule 206(4)-1) controls on any hypothetical/backtested performance shown to investors: policies and procedures, audience-appropriateness, disclosure of criteria/assumptions/risks, 5-year recordkeeping

If firm algorithms trade on EU venues: MiFID II RTS 6 obligations (pre-deployment testing, conformance testing, kill-switch functionality, annual self-assessment); FMSB Statement of Good Practice for applying MRM to electronic trading algorithms as proportionate good practice
Segregation of research and production environments with controlled promotion process
departments.independence: Embedded in the investment function (first line), but material models require validation and effective challenge from a function independent of development and use (model risk / risk management, second line). Research standards-setting can be a shared central service across pods.

handoffs.content: Approved signals and models with documentation, stated limitations, capacity estimates, and expected behavior; usage constraints
Production algorithms and execution assumptions used in backtests; receives inbound fills, slippage, and TCA data to recalibrate cost models
Model inventory entries, documentation packs, and validation artifacts; factor models and exposure feeds; receives inbound validation findings and remediation requirements
Backtested/hypothetical performance with required assumptions, criteria, and risk disclosures for Marketing Rule review before external use
Dataset requirements, point-in-time specifications, and quality SLAs; receives inbound versioned datasets and quality exception reports

handoffs.to: Crypto & On-Chain Research and Technical/Market-Structure teams
handoffs.content: Candidate features and datasets (on-chain flows, positioning, liquidity metrics) submitted with hypothesis documentation for systematic testing under quant governance

regulatoryBasis: Fed SR 11-7 / OCC Bulletin 2011-12 (Supervisory Guidance on Model Risk Management) - formally applies to banking organizations; the de facto template voluntarily adopted by large asset managers
SEC Investment Adviser Marketing Rule, 17 CFR 275.206(4)-1 - binding on registered advisers for hypothetical/backtested performance in advertisements
MiFID II RTS 6 (Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2017/589) and ESMA supervisory briefing on algorithmic trading - binding where in-scope EU algorithmic trading occurs
FMSB Statement of Good Practice: model risk management framework for electronic trading algorithms - industry good practice, not law
practiceNotes: Widely accepted practice: walk-forward testing remains the dominant industry validation method; combinatorial purged cross-validation (Lopez de Prado) is increasingly used but adoption varies

Widely accepted practice: counting trials and deflating Sharpe ratios for selection bias distinguishes institutional-grade research from retail-grade backtesting; the Deflated Sharpe Ratio and PBO are the reference metrics

Judgment call: whether quant research is centralized (Citadel-style, with central research/risk infrastructure) or pod-local (Millennium-style autonomy) varies by firm; centralized standards with pod-level execution is a common hybrid
Judgment call: depth of formal SR 11-7-style validation at hedge funds scales with AUM and institutional-investor expectations; smaller funds substitute peer review plus documented approval

departments.name: Crypto & On-Chain Research
departments.mandate: Produce investable research on digital assets spanning on-chain flow analytics, protocol fundamentals, DeFi risk assessment, token supply/unlock analysis, and counterparty/venue risk, and act as the firm's subject-matter authority for digital-asset market structure and its distinct operational risks.
responsibilities: Build and maintain on-chain data pipelines and entity-tagged flow analytics (exchange inflows/outflows, whale/cohort behavior, stablecoin flows, miner/validator flows)

Protocol fundamental analysis: fee revenue, active users, TVL (with explicit methodology caveats - TVL computation varies across vendors per BIS research), token holder distribution, governance and treasury analysis
Tokenomics and supply-side analysis: emission schedules, vesting cliffs and unlock calendars, float vs fully-diluted valuation, staking/lock-up dynamics, and pre-event notes ahead of material unlocks

DeFi risk assessment: smart-contract risk (audit history, exploit record), oracle risk, composability/interconnectedness risk, liquidation-cascade and depeg scenarios (per FSB/BIS vulnerability taxonomy)

Counterparty and venue risk analysis: exchanges, custodians, OTC desks, lending counterparties - solvency indicators, proof-of-reserves limitations, segregation of client assets, jurisdiction and regulatory status (AIMA DDQ / SBAI ODD frameworks)
Stablecoin and market-plumbing monitoring (reserve composition, redemption mechanics, depeg early-warning)
Track the regulatory landscape (IOSCO recommendations, MiCA, SEC/CFTC developments) and translate implications for tradability, custody, and token classification
Supply curated on-chain datasets and features to Quantitative Research for systematic signal development

workflows.name: Daily on-chain flows and market monitor
workflows.description: Automated dashboard plus analyst commentary on exchange flows, stablecoin issuance/redemption, funding rates, open interest, large-wallet movements, and anomalies; flags actionable items to PMs and risk.
inputs: On-chain analytics vendors and node infrastructure
outputs: Anomaly alerts to PMs and Risk

workflows.name: Protocol/asset coverage notes
workflows.cadence: Weekly publication cycle; full initiation notes event-driven
workflows.description: Fundamental coverage of tokens/protocols under mandate: valuation frameworks, catalyst calendars, governance events, competitive dynamics; initiation notes before any new asset enters the approved trading universe.

workflows.name: Token unlock and supply calendar
workflows.cadence: Weekly refresh; pre-event deep-dives ahead of material unlocks
workflows.description: Maintain a forward calendar of unlocks/vesting cliffs across covered assets, quantify unlock size vs float and average daily volume, assess recipient cohorts' likely behavior, and publish positioning-relevant alerts (vendor research suggests most unlock events create negative price pressure; treat magnitude estimates as vendor-grade, not peer-reviewed).
outputs: Pre-event notes with float/ADV impact estimates

workflows.name: Counterparty and venue review
workflows.cadence: Quarterly formal review; continuous monitoring; ad hoc on stress events
workflows.description: Joint workflow with Operations/Risk: score venues and counterparties on custody arrangements, asset segregation, proof-of-reserves quality, regulatory status, financial disclosures, and operational history; maintain approved venue list and per-venue exposure limits; research provides analysis, independent risk approves.

workflows.name: DeFi protocol risk assessment
workflows.cadence: Event-driven, before capital deployment; re-review on protocol upgrades
workflows.description: Pre-deployment assessment of smart-contract risk, audits, admin-key/upgradeability risk, oracle design, liquidity depth, and composability dependencies; defines maximum deployment size and monitoring triggers.
outputs: Protocol risk assessment with deployment limits

workflows.name: Incident response and depeg/hack monitoring
workflows.description: Playbook-driven response to exchange failures, protocol exploits, stablecoin depegs: assess firm exposure, recommend de-risking, document for post-mortem.

controls: Approved asset universe and approved venue list, with additions requiring documented research plus independent risk/ops sign-off (research recommends, risk approves)
Per-venue and per-protocol exposure limits monitored by risk, informed by research scoring
Custody arrangements following AIMA Digital Asset Custody guide sound practices (segregation, key management, third-party custodian due diligence)
Alignment with SEC examination focus areas for crypto: custody practices, wallet reviews, valuation procedures, BSA/AML compliance touchpoints
Data-vendor validation: reconcile on-chain metrics across sources; document TVL/metric methodology differences (BIS WP 1268 shows TVL is not consistently verifiable across aggregators)
Token legal-classification review handoff to Legal/Compliance before trading new assets (IOSCO 'same activity, same risk, same regulation' direction; MiCA in EU)

Conflict and market-abuse controls for token markets (IOSCO crypto recommendations cover market manipulation, insider trading, conflicts from vertical integration - relevant when dealing with vertically integrated venues)
On-chain research code/data pipelines under the same reproducibility standards as quant research when outputs feed systematic strategies
departments.independence: Embedded research function within the investment organization; however, counterparty/venue approval and exposure limits must have independent second-line (risk/ODD) ownership - research input is advisory to that decision, not determinative. On-chain data engineering can be a shared service with the central data platform.
dependencies: Quantitative Research (systematic testing of on-chain features)

handoffs.content: Asset theses, catalyst/unlock calendars, flow anomaly alerts, DeFi yield opportunity assessments with risk limits
Venue/counterparty risk memos, custody recommendations, protocol risk assessments with proposed exposure limits; receives inbound approved limits and current exposures
Curated, point-in-time on-chain datasets and candidate features with hypothesis documentation, entering quant governance for systematic use
New-asset classification requests, regulatory-development impact notes; receives inbound tradability determinations
Venue execution quality, fill data, and operational incidents feeding venue scoring; outbound liquidity maps for large-order planning

regulatoryBasis: IOSCO Policy Recommendations for Crypto and Digital Asset Markets (FR/11/2023, IOSCOPD747) - regulatory direction for CASPs: conflicts, market abuse, custody, cross-border, operational risk
IOSCO Final Report with Policy Recommendations for Decentralized Finance (IOSCOPD754) - DeFi risk identification and disclosure expectations
FSB report 'The Financial Stability Risks of Decentralised Finance' (Feb 2023) - vulnerability taxonomy: leverage, liquidity/maturity mismatch, interconnectedness, operational fragility
SEC Division of Examinations priorities - crypto custody, wallet reviews, valuation, standards of conduct (binding expectations for registered advisers in scope)
EU MiCA (for EU-facing activity) and evolving SEC/CFTC jurisdiction in the US - firm-specific applicability
AIMA digital asset DDQ and Digital Asset Custody guide; SBAI Operational Due Diligence of Digital Assets toolbox - industry standards, not law
practiceNotes: Widely accepted practice post-FTX: no trading on a venue without documented counterparty review, exposure caps, and preference for off-exchange settlement/third-party custody where available
Widely accepted practice: unlock calendars and float-adjusted supply analysis are table stakes for liquid token strategies; the magnitude of unlock price impact is contested and vendor-research-grade

Judgment call: standalone crypto research department vs embedding crypto coverage in existing asset-class teams - standalone is typical where digital assets are a core strategy; embedded where crypto is an opportunistic sleeve
Judgment call: how much weight to give proof-of-reserves attestations - practice varies; SBAI/AIMA material treats them as one input, not sufficient assurance
Sources disagree on TVL and several on-chain fundamentals: aggregator methodologies differ materially (BIS WP 1268), so institutional practice is to document metric definitions per source

departments.name: Technical & Market-Structure Analysis
departments.mandate: Provide trend/momentum, positioning, liquidity, and market-microstructure intelligence that informs discretionary PM timing and sizing, feeds candidate systematic signals to Quantitative Research, and supports execution decisions - operated as a shared analytical capability rather than an autonomous forecasting desk.
responsibilities: Maintain systematic trend/momentum and price-level monitors across the firm's traded universe (moving-average systems, breadth, relative strength, volatility regimes)
Positioning analysis: weekly CFTC Commitments of Traders processing, dealer gamma estimates, ETF and fund-flow data, futures basis and funding rates, crowding indicators
Liquidity condition monitoring: order-book depth, spreads, volume profiles, intraday liquidity patterns, and stress indicators by market
Market-microstructure research in partnership with the execution/trading function: venue analysis, order-type behavior, transaction-cost model inputs
Regime identification and event-window analysis (e.g., behavior around macro releases, rebalances, expiries) for discretionary PMs
Package durable price-based hypotheses into formal research proposals for Quantitative Research to test under quant governance
Clear labeling of outputs as judgmental commentary vs systematic signal - only the latter enters model governance

workflows.name: Daily technical dashboard and commentary
workflows.description: Automated dashboard of trend state, momentum scores, key levels, volatility regime, and overnight flow observations across covered markets, with brief analyst commentary; distributed to PMs and trading.

workflows.name: Weekly positioning report
workflows.cadence: Weekly (aligned to CFTC COT release, Fridays 3:30 pm ET covering Tuesday data)
workflows.description: Process COT futures-and-options positioning, combine with ETF flows, prime-brokerage crowding data, and dealer-gamma estimates to flag stretched positioning and crowding risk.

workflows.name: Liquidity and market-structure review
workflows.description: Assess liquidity conditions by market (depth, spread, turnover trends), review venue/market-structure changes (tick sizes, auction mechanics, new venues), and update execution assumptions with the trading desk.

workflows.name: Signal candidate handoff to Quantitative Research
workflows.description: Technical hypotheses that appear durable (e.g., a positioning-conditioned momentum filter) are written up with an explicit testable hypothesis and handed to quant research for out-of-sample testing under the trial registry - the technical team does not self-certify signals into production.
outputs: Formal research proposals with hypothesis documentation

workflows.name: Event and regime notes
workflows.description: Short notes around index rebalances, option expiries, seasonal patterns, and stress episodes describing expected flow/liquidity effects for PMs and trading.

controls: Documented methodology for every published indicator (definition, data source, lookback, known limitations) - prevents 'chart lore' from circulating as research

Governance boundary: any technical output used systematically in production must pass through quant research's model governance (trial registry, out-of-sample testing, model documentation) - commentary alone never trades
Positioning/crowding alerts routed to risk management with defined thresholds
Marketing Rule discipline: technical projections/targets are not used in investor-facing materials without compliance review (projected performance is 'hypothetical performance' under Rule 206(4)-1)
Data licensing compliance for exchange/order-book data
Clear separation between market commentary (judgmental) and signals (governed) in all distribution channels
departments.independence: Embedded / shared service. Institutional consensus practice at multi-strategy funds is NOT to run technical analysis as a standalone forecasting desk: trend/momentum is industrialized inside quantitative/systematic strategies (the CTA/managed-futures literature treats it as a factor), microstructure research sits with or alongside execution/trading, and positioning/liquidity intelligence works best as a central shared capability serving PMs and risk. Standalone 'technical strategist' roles persist mainly on the sell side (research product) and at some macro funds - a firm-level judgment call, not a standard.
dependencies: Market data infrastructure (price, order book, derivatives)
Quantitative Research (testing and governance of candidate signals)
Risk management (positions for crowding overlap analysis)

handoffs.content: Daily technical dashboards, positioning/crowding reports, event notes - explicitly labeled as context/commentary, not governed signals
Formally written candidate hypotheses (indicator definition, economic rationale, expected behavior) entering the trial registry for out-of-sample testing
Liquidity assessments, execution-window recommendations, market-structure change alerts; receives inbound TCA and order-book analytics
Crowding and stretched-positioning alerts against defined thresholds; regime-change flags relevant to scenario selection

regulatoryBasis: No regulation mandates or governs technical analysis itself; applicable rules are indirect: SEC Marketing Rule for any projected/backtested performance shown externally; market-abuse regimes constrain trading on certain flow information; data licensing terms for exchange data
CFTC Commitments of Traders reports - official public positioning data source (weekly, Friday 3:30 pm ET, Tuesday's open interest)
MiFID II RTS 6 applies if technical rules are automated into EU algorithmic trading
practiceNotes: Widely accepted: trend and momentum are documented return factors (CTA/managed-futures literature, CFA Institute publications decompose CTA returns into trend horizons); their institutional home is systematic strategies, not chart-reading desks
Widely accepted: positioning data (COT, PB crowding, dealer gamma) is consumed firm-wide; many multi-strats centralize it in a 'market intelligence' or central risk function
Judgment call: discretionary macro funds sometimes retain a dedicated technician (CMT-credentialed); equity multi-strats almost never do - varies by strategy mix

Judgment call: whether microstructure research reports into trading (execution focus) or quant research (alpha focus); larger firms often split it - execution research under trading, microstructure alpha under quant

structuralRecommendations: Keep Quantitative Research as a distinct first-line department, but stand up independent model validation in risk (second line) with effective-challenge authority - the SR 11-7 separation of development from validation is the defining institutional-grade control

Create a shared Research Data & Platform function (point-in-time data, backtest infrastructure, trial registry) serving all three research areas rather than letting each build its own - reproducibility is a platform property, not a team habit

Run Crypto & On-Chain Research as a standalone department only if digital assets are a core strategy; either way, move venue/counterparty APPROVAL out of research into independent risk/ODD - research recommends, risk approves

Do not create a standalone Technical Analysis desk; constitute Technical & Market-Structure Analysis as a shared 'market intelligence' capability, with durable price-based hypotheses funneled into quant research governance and microstructure work partnered with execution

Establish a single firm-wide research review committee and model inventory spanning all three areas, so crypto/on-chain and technical signals face the same overfitting and documentation gates as traditional quant signals
Route all externally shared backtested/hypothetical performance from any of the three teams through one compliance workflow implementing SEC Marketing Rule requirements
disagreementsAndVariation: SR 11-7 formally binds banking organizations only; buy-side firms adopt it voluntarily and the depth of formal validation varies strongly with AUM and allocator pressure - smaller funds substitute documented peer review

Validation methodology: walk-forward testing remains the practical industry standard, while the academic literature (Lopez de Prado et al.) argues combinatorial purged cross-validation better controls overfitting; firms genuinely differ here

Technical analysis org placement: sell-side institutions still publish standalone technical strategy; buy-side multi-strats overwhelmingly embed it (systematic momentum or PM-level toolkit); some macro funds keep dedicated technicians - no single consensus

Centralization vs pod autonomy in research: Citadel-style centralized research/risk infrastructure vs Millennium-style independent pods are both successful operating models; the choice drives how much of quant research is a shared service

Crypto data quality: on-chain metrics (notably TVL) are computed inconsistently across aggregators (BIS WP 1268); entity-tagging accuracy of flow analytics differs by vendor - conclusions built on one vendor's labels are contestable

Token unlock impact: the claim that ~90% of unlock events create negative price pressure comes from vendor/industry research, not peer-reviewed literature; direction is widely believed, magnitude is disputed
Proof-of-reserves: treated as useful but insufficient evidence of venue solvency in industry ODD material; some firms weight it more heavily than others
Crypto regulatory perimeter remains unsettled between SEC and CFTC in the US; IOSCO's 2025 thematic review shows uneven jurisdictional implementation of its 2023 recommendations

sourcesConsulted.whatItSupported: Primary source (fetched): SR 11-7 model definition, development standards, independent validation and effective challenge, outcomes analysis, documentation, model inventory, governance roles
Bailey & Lopez de Prado, Deflated Sharpe Ratio - correcting for selection bias under multiple testing and non-normality; basis for trial-registry overfitting controls
Full text of the Deflated Sharpe Ratio paper (author-hosted)

IOSCO Policy Recommendations for Crypto and Digital Asset Markets (2023): conflicts, market abuse, custody/client asset protection, operational risk - regulatory direction for venue/counterparty controls
IOSCO Final Report with Policy Recommendations for DeFi (2023): DeFi risk identification, disclosures, cross-border cooperation
IOSCO 2025 thematic review - uneven implementation of crypto recommendations across jurisdictions (supports 'regulatory perimeter unsettled' variation note)
AIMA digital asset DDQs covering strategy, trading, risk, custody, valuation - industry standard for crypto fund/counterparty due diligence
AIMA Digital Asset Custody guide - sound practices for institutional custody due diligence
SBAI toolbox on operational due diligence of digital assets (URL as indexed; found via search as sbai.org static PDF) - ODD criteria for venues/custody
FSB 'Financial Stability Risks of Decentralised Finance' (2023) - DeFi vulnerability taxonomy: leverage, liquidity mismatch, interconnectedness, operational fragility
BIS Quarterly Review 'DeFi risks and the decentralisation illusion' - governance concentration and DeFi risk framing
BIS Working Paper 1268 on verifiability of TVL - basis for treating TVL/on-chain metrics as methodology-dependent
Text of SEC Marketing Rule 206(4)-1 - definition and conditions for hypothetical (incl. backtested and projected) performance
Law-firm analysis of hypothetical performance requirements: policies/procedures, audience relevance, disclosure of criteria/assumptions/risks
FMSB Statement of Good Practice - proportionate application of model risk management to electronic trading algorithms, model risk tiering
MiFID II RTS 6 text - algorithm testing, kill-switch, annual self-assessment obligations for in-scope algorithmic trading
ESMA supervisory briefing on algorithmic trading (incl. AI/ML in algos) - current EU supervisory expectations
Official CFTC Commitments of Traders reports - weekly positioning data, Friday 3:30 pm ET release covering Tuesday open interest

CFA Institute Research Foundation literature review 'Technical Analysis: Modern Perspectives' (Hill, Nadig, Hougan) - located as evidence of institutional-grade TA literature; PDF content could not be machine-extracted, so no detailed claims are attributed to it
CFA Institute Enterprising Investor - decomposition of CTA returns into trend horizons; supports treating trend/momentum as an industrialized systematic factor
SEC Division of Examinations priorities - crypto custody practices, wallet reviews, valuation procedures, standards of conduct for advisers
Multi-strategy fund operating model: decentralized pods within centralized risk controls
Practice literature on pod-shop structures and centralized vs decentralized research/risk (Citadel vs Millennium contrast)
Backtesting pitfalls and out-of-sample discipline (look-ahead, survivorship, data snooping) - textbook treatment corroborating standards list
Comparison of out-of-sample testing methods: combinatorial purged CV vs walk-forward; supports the 'validation methodology varies' disagreement note

confidence: medium-high: quant research standards and model-risk governance rest on primary regulatory texts (SR 11-7, Marketing Rule, RTS 6) - high confidence; crypto research controls rest on strong industry-body material (IOSCO, FSB, BIS, AIMA, SBAI) - medium-high; organizational placement of technical analysis is a well-signposted judgment call with thinner primary sourcing - medium
